question:
Where can we learn about Cummins' efforts in community engagement and social responsibility?
Select titles from the doc that best answer the question, do not alter or analyze the titles themselves.
answer: COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT // COMMUNITY IMPACT AND GIVING

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment. A box notes that Cummins’ CARE initiative, launched in 2020, focuses on police reform, criminal justice reform, and social justice.</abstract></node>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>BUILDING MORE PROSPEROUS COMMUNITIES</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:5:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>PRIORITY AREAS section heading, item 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:7:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>EDUCATION section heading, followed by the number 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>ENVIRONMENT section heading, numbered 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment. A box notes that Cummins’ CARE initiative, launched in 2020, focuses on police reform, criminal justice reform, and social justice.</abstract></node>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:11:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>EQUALITY OF OPPORTUNITY section heading.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:5:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>PRIORITY AREAS section heading, item 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:9:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>ENVIRONMENT section heading, numbered 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34:block:10:title" type="title" page_index="34"><abstract>TO LEARN MORE 1</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment. A box notes that Cummins’ CARE initiative, launched in 2020, focuses on police reform, criminal justice reform, and social justice.</abstract></node>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment. A box notes that Cummins’ CARE initiative, launched in 2020, focuses on police reform, criminal justice reform, and social justice.</abstract></node>
    </active_nodes>
    <opened_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="31"><abstract>BUILDING MORE PROSPEROUS COMMUNITIES</abstract><content truncated="false">BUILDING MORE PROSPEROUS COMMUNITIES</content><bbox>[58, 156, 742, 210]</bbox></node>

    <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:16:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Stephen Layton, a Cummins telecommunications manager in the United Kingdom, used skills from a previous job to volunteer testing essential medical gases in emergency care centers.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:18:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees in Wuxi, China, assisted a local school serving migrant students in cleaning before students returned after a pandemic shutdown.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:20:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employee Stephen Aryee used analytical skills to develop modeling for an Indiana (U.S.) hospital to predict peaks in COVID-19 admissions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Each Cummins CEO since the 1950s has personally committed to corporate responsibility and community strength; employees can choose non-profits and fund projects via Cummins Community Development grants.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment.     <active_nodes>
      <node id="4004473:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Page 30 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report discusses advocacy and partnerships as critical to Cummins’ environmental strategy and compliance. The company advocates for tough, clear, and enforceable regulations and partners to develop technologies to meet them, including working with the U.S. EPA and California Air Resources Board. Cummins is also involved in multiple partnerships, such as the California Hydrogen Business Council and the Green Power Partnership, to promote low-carbon solutions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:29:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="29"><abstract>Heading: ADVOCACY, PARTNERSHIPS CRITICAL TO CUMMINS PROGRESS</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31" type="page" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees are deeply committed to building more prosperous communities worldwide through volunteer efforts and supported initiatives. The page highlights three priority areas: education, environment, and equality of opportunity. Key 2020 metrics include $22 million in community grants, 1.4 million people served, 17 million women and girls impacted by policy changes, and 1,700 students graduating from Cummins TEC: Technical Education for Communities.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:16:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Stephen Layton, a Cummins telecommunications manager in the United Kingdom, used skills from a previous job to volunteer testing essential medical gases in emergency care centers.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:18:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employees in Wuxi, China, assisted a local school serving migrant students in cleaning before students returned after a pandemic shutdown.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:20:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Cummins employee Stephen Aryee used analytical skills to develop modeling for an Indiana (U.S.) hospital to predict peaks in COVID-19 admissions.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:31:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="31"><abstract>Each Cummins CEO since the 1950s has personally committed to corporate responsibility and community strength; employees can choose non-profits and fund projects via Cummins Community Development grants.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:33" type="page" page_index="33"><abstract>Cummins has developed two strategic community initiatives to address large, global problems it can uniquely help solve. The first, Cummins TEC, launched in 2012, trains disadvantaged youth through technical vocational education. Today, the program operates in 24 countries and has graduated over 1,700 students, with 80% securing good jobs. In 2020, despite pandemic-related disruptions, enrollment exceeded its 2020 goal by 10% through increased female participation.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34" type="page" page_index="34"><abstract>Salvador Diliz, General Manager Cummins Colombia, and Eliana Scarpatti, Health, Safety, Environment and Quality leader, meet with domestic workers prior to the pandemic as part of Cummins Powers Women nonprofit partner CARE. Cummins TEC was followed in 2018 by Cummins Powers Women, a $20 million global community program to advance equity for women and girls. The program partners with eight global nonprofits to accelerate gender equality in education, economic empowerment, safety, and legal rights.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:34:block:7:title" type="title" page_index="34"><abstract>CUMMINS POWERS WOMEN PARTNERS 1</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40" type="page" page_index="40"><abstract>Page 41 of the 2020 Cummins Sustainability Progress Report details the company’s DE&I strategy under the heading “A History of Commitment.” It highlights global initiatives focused on gender and military service, led by the Global Inclusion Leadership Council. The page also outlines key strategy elements including leadership accountability, recruitment and advancement, benefits and compensation, learning and education, community and social responsibility, assessment and research, and communications, all aimed at creating a diverse, equitable, and inclusive environment. A box notes that Cummins’ CARE initiative, launched in 2020, focuses on police reform, criminal justice reform, and social justice.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40:block:18:title" type="title" page_index="40"><abstract>LEARNING AND EDUCATION: section heading with number 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4004473:page:40:block:20:title" type="title" page_index="40"><abstract>Heading: COMMUNITY, GOVERNMENT AND SOCIAL RESPONSIBILITY.</abstract></node>

Management and supervisory board members have indirect or no direct ownership.</abstract></node>

      <node id="4088207:page:13" type="page" page_index="13"><abstract>Dividend policy states payments are decided annually based on Group performance and investment needs, determined by a general meeting resolution. Dividends or advances are distributed proportionally among shareholders up to half the expected profit, fixed on the 10th trading day after the general meeting.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:13:block:0:title" type="title" page_index="13"><abstract>DIVIDEND POLICY section heading, numbered 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14" type="page" page_index="14"><abstract>Management Board monitors cash flow using financial resource sufficiency for obligations and strategic financing. Group structure section lists subsidiaries: Nordic Fibreboard Ltd OÜ, Skano Furniture OÜ, Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ, OÜ Isotex, and Suomen Tuulileijona OY, with ownership and share counts as of 31.12.2020 and 31.12.2019. Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ was established in 2019 via merger and remains dormant; OÜ Isotex is non-active with no business activities in 2020 and 2019.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14:block:1:title" type="title" page_index="14"><abstract>GROUP STRUCTURE section heading, numbered 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:15" type="page" page_index="15"><abstract>The General Meeting of Shareholders of Nordic Fibreboard AS held on August 10, 2020, was attended by members of the Management Board and the Chairman of the Supervisory Board; the auditor and most Supervisory Board members were absent. The auditor’s report was not considered, as no material issues were raised regarding the financial statements. Shareholders had the opportunity to ask questions via phone.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:16" type="page" page_index="16"><abstract>During 2020, the Supervisory Board of the Issuer did not establish any committees. The Issuer does not pay remuneration to Supervisory Board members and does not disclose remuneration details or meeting dates publicly. The company’s environmental policy, including permits for waste generation and mineral resource activities, is outlined on this page.</abstract></node>

      <node id="4088207:page:12" type="page" page_index="12"><abstract>Share capital as of 31.12.2020: Estonia holds 94% of shareholders and 95% of shares; other countries including Finland, Germany, Latvia, and Lithuania each hold small percentages. Management and supervisory board members have indirect or no direct ownership.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:12:block:2:table" type="table" page_index="12"><abstract>Table lists shareholders with ownership exceeding 1% as of 31.12.2020, showing their names, number of shares, and corresponding shareholding percentages.       <node id="4088207:page:12:block:5:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="12"><abstract>Joakim Johan Helenius and Torfinn Losvik have indirect ownership via Pärnu Holdings OÜ; Torfinn Losvik owns 44,206 shares through Stetind OÜ (same as 2019).</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:13" type="page" page_index="13"><abstract>Dividend policy states payments are decided annually based on Group performance and investment needs, determined by a general meeting resolution.       <node id="4088207:page:14" type="page" page_index="14"><abstract>Management Board monitors cash flow using financial resource sufficiency for obligations and strategic financing. Group structure section lists subsidiaries: Nordic Fibreboard Ltd OÜ, Skano Furniture OÜ, Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ, OÜ Isotex, and Suomen Tuulileijona OY, with ownership and share counts as of 31.12.2020 and 31.12.2019. Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ was established in 2019 via merger and remains dormant; OÜ Isotex is non-active with no business activities in 2020 and 2019.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14:block:1:title" type="title" page_index="14"><abstract>GROUP STRUCTURE section heading, numbered 1.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:15" type="page" page_index="15"><abstract>The General Meeting of Shareholders of Nordic Fibreboard AS held on August 10, 2020, was attended by members of the Management Board and the Chairman of the Supervisory Board; the auditor and most Supervisory Board members were absent. The auditor’s report was not considered, as no material issues were raised regarding the financial statements. Shareholders had the opportunity to ask questions via phone.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:16" type="page" page_index="16"><abstract>During 2020, the Supervisory Board of the Issuer did not establish any committees. The Issuer does not pay remuneration to Supervisory Board members and does not disclose remuneration details or meeting dates publicly.       <node id="4088207:page:12" type="page" page_index="12"><abstract>Share capital as of 31.12.2020: Estonia holds 94% of shareholders and 95% of shares; other countries including Finland, Germany, Latvia, and Lithuania each hold small percentages. Management and supervisory board members have indirect or no direct ownership.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:13" type="page" page_index="13"><abstract>Dividend policy states payments are decided annually based on Group performance and investment needs, determined by a general meeting resolution. Dividends or advances are distributed proportionally among shareholders up to half the expected profit, fixed on the 10th trading day after the general meeting.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14" type="page" page_index="14"><abstract>Management Board monitors cash flow using financial resource sufficiency for obligations and strategic financing.       <node id="4088207:page:16" type="page" page_index="16"><abstract>During 2020, the Supervisory Board of the Issuer did not establish any committees.       <node id="4088207:page:12" type="page" page_index="12"><abstract>Share capital as of 31.12.2020: Estonia holds 94% of shareholders and 95% of shares; other countries including Finland, Germany, Latvia, and Lithuania each hold small percentages. Total shareholders: 485; total shares: 4,499,061. Top shareholders include Pärnu Holdings OÜ (60%) and Gamma Holding Investment OÜ (8%). Management and supervisory board members have indirect or no direct ownership.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:13" type="page" page_index="13"><abstract>Dividend policy states payments are decided annually based on Group performance and investment needs, determined by a general meeting resolution. Dividends or advances are distributed proportionally among shareholders up to half the expected profit, fixed on the 10th trading day after the general meeting.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:13:block:1:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="13"><abstract>Dividends are decided annually based on Group performance and investment needs, determined by a general meeting resolution; advance payments up to half the expected profit may be made before annual report approval with supervisory board consent, and distributions are proportional according to the shareholder list fixed on the 10th trading day after the decision.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14" type="page" page_index="14"><abstract>Management Board monitors cash flow using financial resource sufficiency for obligations and strategic financing. Group structure section lists subsidiaries: Nordic Fibreboard Ltd OÜ, Skano Furniture OÜ, Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ, OÜ Isotex, and Suomen Tuulileijona OY, with ownership and share counts as of 31.12.2020 and 31.12.2019. Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ was established in 2019 via merger and remains dormant; OÜ Isotex is non-active with no business activities in 2020 and 2019.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:14:block:2:table" type="table" page_index="14"><abstract>The table reports the number of shares and ownership percentage for six subsidiaries as of December 31, 2020, and December 31, 2019.       <node id="4088207:page:14:block:3:paragraph" type="paragraph" page_index="14"><abstract>Nordic Fibreboard AS holds subsidiaries Nordic Fibreboard Ltd OÜ, Pärnu Riverside Development OÜ (established by demerger since 2019), and Skano Furniture OÜ. Nordic Fibreboard Ltd OÜ manufactures and distributes wood fibreboards for construction, interior panels, and industrial applications, and owns subsidiary Suomen Tuulielijona OY (Finland marketing company). Skano Furniture OÜ owns dormant subsidiary Skano LT UAB (ceased operations September 2019).</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:15" type="page" page_index="15"><abstract>The General Meeting of Shareholders of Nordic Fibreboard AS held on August 10, 2020, was attended by members of the Management Board and the Chairman of the Supervisory Board; the auditor and most Supervisory Board members were absent. The auditor’s report was not considered, as no material issues were raised regarding the financial statements. Shareholders had the opportunity to ask questions via phone.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4088207:page:16" type="page" page_index="16"><abstract>During 2020, the Supervisory Board of the Issuer did not establish any committees. The Issuer does not pay remuneration to Supervisory Board members and does not disclose remuneration details or meeting dates publicly.       <node id="4028159:page:21" type="page" page_index="21"><abstract>Page lists permits from La Habra City and Section 6 on environmental factors, noting at least one potential “Potentially Significant Impact” as indicated in Table 1. Environmental analysis concludes the project may have such an impact, requiring an Environmental Impact Report.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:23" type="page" page_index="23"><abstract>Table 2, Initial Study Checklist, lists environmental and cultural resource impacts for a project in Volara Townhomes, LA Habra, California. It categorizes issues under “Potentially Significant Impact,” “Less than Significant Impact with Mitigation,” and “No Impact,” with “X” marks indicating assessed impacts. Section 3.4 (Biological Resources) and Section 3.5 (Cultural Resources) contain specific checklist items, with “X” marks indicating “Less than Significant Impact” or “No Impact” for most entries.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:24" type="page" page_index="24"><abstract>Table 2 Initial Study Checklist on page 25 lists evaluation criteria for project impacts under sections 3.6 (Energy), 3.7 (Geology and Soils), and 3.8 (Greenhouse Gas Emissions). For each issue, it indicates whether evaluation in an EIR is required or if the impact is classified as “Less than Significant Impact” or “No Impact,” with “X” marks denoting the selected category.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:25" type="page" page_index="25"><abstract>Table 2, titled “Initial Study Checklist,” assesses potential environmental impacts under two sections: Section 3.9 (Hazards and Hazardous Materials) and Section 3.10 (Hydrology and Water Quality). For Section 3.9, items 3.9.A through 3.9.G are marked with “X” under “Less than Significant Impact with Mitigation.” For Section 3.10, items 3.10.A and 3.10.B are marked with “X” under “Less than Significant Impact.” The table’s columns include “Potentially Significant Impact,” “Less than Significant Impact with Mitigation,” and “No Impact.”</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:29" type="page" page_index="29"><abstract>Table 2, titled “Initial Study Checklist,” lists environmental impact evaluation criteria. It includes issues such as wildfire risk exposure, infrastructure installation, and slope instability, each marked with “X” under “Less than Significant Impact.” Section 3.21 details mandatory findings of significance, with three sub-items marked “X” indicating potential environmental impacts. Section 8, “Environmental Analysis,” lists evaluated issue areas including Aesthetics, Agriculture and Forestry Resources, Air Quality, Biological Resources, Mineral Resources, Noise, Population and Housing, and Public Services.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:29:block:0:table" type="table" page_index="29"><abstract>Table 2 Initial Study Checklist reports on three issues (3.20.B, 3.20.C, 3.20.D) and indicates for each that they are classified as “Less than Significant Impact,” marked with an “X” in the corresponding column, while leaving the “Requires Evaluation in EIR” columns blank.</abstract></node>
      <node id="4028159:page:29:block:1:table" type="table" page_index="29"><abstract>Table reports mandatory findings of significance for a project, with rows 3.21.A, 3.21.B, and 3.21.C each marked with an "X" indicating the project meets the specified criteria.</abstract></node>
